citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
During the inspection, a facility representative stated they are still only using soap and water to clean the food and water
receptacles, without any sanitization. Upon consultation with the attending veterinarian, she stated that food and water
receptacles should be cleaned and sanitized routinely for prevention of diseases and was not aware of the continued
failure to sanitize. When the veterinarian’s instructions are not followed, the provision of veterinary care and/or husbandry
may be inadequate or inappropriate. The facility needs to follow the instructions provided by the attending veterinarian
and as directed in the written program of veterinary care.

The following indoor facility areas were not kept in good repair within Room 5:
• On inspection, two juvenile ferrets from the top enclosure, within a tier of three, were observed outside and below their
enclosure. They had gained access below the plastic mesh floor of the enclosure and were found standing in their plastic
waste collection tray. The ferrets were able to raise the plastic mesh floor in such a way that allowed their escape due to
the floor not being secure. Corrections were initiated by adding metal rods below the plastic mesh floor to stabilize and
hold the floor in place.
• In another enclosure housing one ferret, a plastic shelf tray had fallen from its elevated position and was lying on the
enclosure floor, slanted up with the right corner raised, exposing multiple cracks and breaks in the plastic creating sharp
points and space (ranging from approximately 1/4" to 1 "in width) where a ferret’s body part could get entrapped. The
plastic shelf was removed during the inspection.
Failure to maintain the enclosures in good repair may allow for escape and/or cause harm to the animals by allowing
injury, or discomfort from the exposed sharp points and gaps. The enclosure must be structurally sound and shall be
maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals.
Corrected during this inspection, and shall remain corrected from March 4, 2026, forward.

Housekeeping within the indoor ferret housing room was inadequate in Room 3. The room had brown residue and/or
stains running down the walls located behind the towers of the primary enclosures. Discarded feed was found
accumulating within the wall air conditioning vents. An excess of discarded feed and excrement was found on the floor
and within the corners of the room. Discarded feed and/or excrement on the walls and other structures within the rooms
housing ferrets may expose the animals to disease hazards, odors, and/or attract pests. The facility shall ensure the
housing rooms, including the wall a/c units and their vents, walls, floors, and other structures within the rooms, are kept
clean by removing accumulations of feces and spilled feed as necessary to protect the health of the animals and to
facilitate husbandry practices.
Correct by: 03/15/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
During the inspection, a facility representative stated they are still only using soap and water to clean the food and water 
receptacles, without any sanitization. Upon consultation with the attending veterinarian, she stated that food and water 
receptacles should be cleaned and sanitized routinely for prevention of diseases and was not aware of the continued 
failure to sanitize. When the veterinarian’s instructions are not followed, the provision of veterinary care and/or husbandry 
may be inadequate or inappropriate. The facility needs to follow the instructions provided by the attending veterinarian 
and as directed in the written program of veterinary care. 
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
The following indoor facility areas were not kept in good repair within Room 5:  
• On inspection, two juvenile ferrets from the top enclosure, within a tier of three, were observed outside and below their 
enclosure. They had gained access below the plastic mesh floor of the enclosure and were found standing in their plastic 
waste collection tray. The ferrets were able to raise the plastic mesh floor in such a way that allowed their escape due to 
the floor not being secure. Corrections were initiated by adding metal rods below the plastic mesh floor to stabilize and 
hold the floor in place. 
• In another enclosure housing one ferret, a plastic shelf tray had fallen from its elevated position and was lying on the 
enclosure floor, slanted up with the right corner raised, exposing multiple cracks and breaks in the plastic creating sharp 
points and space (ranging from approximately 1/4" to 1 "in width) where a ferret’s body part could get entrapped. The 
plastic shelf was removed during the inspection.  
Failure to maintain the enclosures in good repair may allow for escape and/or cause harm to the animals by allowing 
injury, or discomfort from the exposed sharp points and gaps. The enclosure must be structurally sound and shall be 
maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals. 
Corrected during this inspection, and shall remain corrected from March 4, 2026, forward. 
3.131(c) 
   
Sanitation. 
Housekeeping within the indoor ferret housing room was inadequate in Room 3. The room had brown residue and/or 
stains running down the walls located behind the towers of the primary enclosures. Discarded feed was found

accumulating within the wall air conditioning vents. An excess of discarded feed and excrement was found on the floor 
and within the corners of the room. Discarded feed and/or excrement on the walls and other structures within the rooms 
housing ferrets may expose the animals to disease hazards, odors, and/or attract pests. The facility shall ensure the 
housing rooms, including the wall a/c units and their vents, walls, floors, and other structures within the rooms, are kept 
clean by removing accumulations of feces and spilled feed as necessary to protect the health of the animals and to 
facilitate husbandry practices. 
Correct by: 03/15/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
